title: Preparing willow for basketwork
subtitle: Soaking times, rod grading, and storing the three commercial willow types
excerpt: Brown, buff, and white willow are the three commercial preparations of basketry willow; each with different soaking times, working properties, and finished appearance. This guide covers what each preparation is, how to soak it correctly, how to select rods by size for different basket types, and how to store dry willow between projects.
sourceNotes: Traditional British commercial basketry practice; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for willow preparation and grading method.

glossaryTerms.definition: Willow rods dried after harvest without further treatment; the natural bark is retained and the colour ranges from dark brown to pale gold. Needs 1 to 2 hours soaking before use.
Willow rods harvested, retted (soaked), and steamed to produce a warm tan colour. The steam process kills the bark, making the rods paler and slightly softer. Needs 30 minutes soaking.
Willow rods stripped of bark after harvest; pale cream colour and the most uniformly flexible of the commercial preparations. Needs 1 hour soaking.

content.text: Dry basketry willow is not usable until it has been re-soaked to restore the flexibility it had when green. The three commercial preparations;

; differ in how the bark has been treated and require different soaking times. Getting the soaking right is the single biggest variable in whether willow work goes smoothly or fights you throughout the project.

Brown willow: submerge in cold water for 1 to 2 hours. After soaking, remove and wrap in a damp cloth or hessian for 30 minutes before working; this allows the moisture to equalise through the rod rather than sitting only on the surface. Buff willow: 30 minutes submerged; 20 minutes in the cloth. White willow: 1 hour submerged; 30 minutes in the cloth. The rule for all three: the rods are ready when they flex through 180 degrees around a 10 cm diameter cylinder without cracking or snapping at the bend. Under-soaked rods crack at sharp bends and are difficult to weave around stakes. Over-soaked rods lose stiffness and the weave does not hold tension correctly.

Commercial willow is sold by length (in 30 cm increments from 60 cm to 270 cm) and the diameter tapers naturally from butt to tip. A 90 cm rod is suitable for weavers in a small basket; a 180 cm rod provides weavers for a log basket or sides of a large project. Stakes (the uprights) should be 2 to 3 mm wider in diameter than the weavers; a stake that is the same thickness as the weaver provides insufficient structure. Sort rods by size before starting any project: weavers in one group, stakes in another. Mixing sizes in the same group produces uneven tension throughout the weave.

Brown willow is the standard choice for functional baskets; its rough bark provides grip, it is the strongest of the three preparations, and it is typically less expensive. Buff willow is preferred for decorative work where a consistent warm colour matters; gift baskets, willow wreaths, seasonal decorations. White willow is the choice for very fine work (small baskets, intricate borders) because the peeled surface accepts dye evenly and the pale colour shows pattern detail clearly. For a first basket, brown willow is the best starting material: the bark helps identify the direction of the weave and the rough texture is forgiving of uneven tension.

Store dry willow in bundles, horizontally, in a cool dry place with some air movement. Damp storage causes mould on the bark surface, which stains the rod and weakens it. Do not store in sealed plastic; the residual moisture in a recently purchased bundle needs to escape before storage. Dry willow stored correctly remains usable for several years; the soaking time may increase slightly with age as the rods dry further, but the working properties are unchanged.

Freshly harvested green willow (cut from the tree in winter or early spring) requires no soaking and is the most flexible of all. It is easier to weave than soaked dry willow and works well for large outdoor items; hurdles, garden structures, decorative arches. The limitation is that green willow is only available in late winter and spring, and it must be used within a week of cutting before it dries and stiffens in the air. Green willow is the best choice for a first basket if it can be sourced locally at the right time.
